Bonus QA – Step 5: The Grand Offer
All Parts (Extended)
ALL CONTENT © COPYRIGHT 2011 AND BEYOND | JON BENSON 3X

Jon: 	Okay, let’s get into the 30-minute optional question and answer
session for the massive step 5 of the 5-step VSL process, the grand
offer. Josh, take it away.
Josh: 	Alright, so Leslie has our first question, and she’s asking, “Whenever
you were talking about reviewing the sales page underneath the VSL,
then is the button far below the bottom of the page, or is it at the top,
above the long-form sales page?”
Jon: 	I’m sorry, Josh. Can you ask that one more time?
Josh: 	Sure. Whenever you have a reveal, the button revealed, you said you
can also reveal a sales page underneath. Leslie wants to know if the
sales page is revealed underneath, do you have the button all the way
at the bottom?
Jon: 	You have a button at the top and a button at the bottom. For that
matter, you can have buy buttons wherever you want them. But I have
one at the top and bottom, for sure.
Josh: 	Okay, Leslie has several options for her product, we talked about
before. She wanted to know would it be okay, if you wanted to test
this, to have a different sales page appear after whatever, depending
on what choice they make? Meaning if they want to learn more about
one, a new sales page appears for that thing, and so on.
Jon: 	That’s going to be a call you’ll have to make by testing. There’s no
right or wrong answer. The thing that I like to do is either have the one
button that goes to the options, and you can have all the options, if

-- 1 of 15 --

2
they’re short enough, on the actual order form.
Don’t forget, you can have an order that looks like a sales page. So
one thing that’s very cool to do is to have your site hosted on a https
domain, which means it’s a secure socket layer. That means that you
have to buy an SSL certificate, which costs about $60. Then you have
someone that knows what they’re doing set it up on your system,
which takes about 3 minutes.
Then after that, you can collect credit card data on your system, if you
have a merchant that allows you to have their form sitting on your
website.
Infusion, as much as they suck, they do allow you to have that. I mean
they totally suck, but it’s kind of like there’s not many alternatives out
there. The positive thing about them is that they do allow you to have
their sales page, their order form on any page.
So, in other words, picture it this way. You could toggle down and you
wouldn’t have a buy button, if you have several options. You can say,
“Look beneath and choose the option that fits best for you.” And you
could have literally option #1, blank, and have 500 words a copy;
option #2, blank, 500 words a copy. And then buy, buy, buy.
The cool thing about that is if you click buy, you could just basically
have an HTML command that takes them down to the order form. You
could have the order form sitting on the same page, or you could go to
a different page. But I’m just giving some suggestions.
I hope that answers the question.
Josh: 	Okay. Next, Timmy wanted to know you didn’t have a dollar value in
the reminder slide. Did you do that on purpose, or was that something
you left out on accident?
Jon: 	Nope, it’s either way. You can remind them of the value of it. But, at
this point, I’m more interested in reminding them of benefits. They
already know there’s a value, because there’s that one slide where I
said, “Don’t forget, other than this great discount…” That’s really all I
said. I’m more keen on giving them the issues of the value. But, like I
said, that’s not a right or wrong answer either way.
This is one of the cool things about this course, is that somebody might
say, “You know what I did? I tested putting dollar values in there and
increased conversions 10%.” Awesome, yeah. So, knock yourself out.
I’d do it.
Josh: 	Okay. Next, Ken said that he was watching one of your videos, at one

-- 2 of 15 --

3
point, presumably on another sales page, and he said the opt-in box
stayed on the screen as he scrolled down the page. He wanted to
know how you did that.
Jon: 	I’ll never tell. Yeah, I know which video he used. Well, again, that’s
Java script. You were looking at a video that was for an opt-in page
that we did for a client, and we built a Java script that enabled the opt-
in box to follow you as you scrolled down the screen on the far right,
which is kind of cool.
But that’s Java script, and my best answer for that would be to go to
that page, look at the source code. I didn’t write the Java script, the
programmer did. But we did it through Java script.
And if you tell a programmer, this is stuff that a programmer with any
decent skills can do in a few hours. It’s going to cost you $100 to have
it done. So, hope that helps.
Josh: 	Cool. Alright, so Heather asks, she says, for the section where the

audio, she loved those. Those are great. She said, “Should you,” and
I’m really curious about this, “could you actually keep the call to action
on the screen while you put those there?”
Jon: 	A call to action is always on the screen. Oh, my bad. I was thinking
magic buy button. My bad.
Yes, yes, you certainly could. You certainly could. There’s not a
problem with that. Like you could put just the same call to action and
just have a section blotted out in the center of that video. You’d have
to make some things smaller, obviously. But, yeah, that would be a
cool way to do it. Sure. In fact, that’s not a bad idea. I think I’ll try that
myself. Thanks.
Josh: 	Yeah, I was just thinking that’s pretty cool. Okay, so this is from Leslie,
and Leslie says most of the traffic that they get is from SEO.
Obviously, you can’t really have a good sales video on a page that you
want to be __ (6:06) for SEO. You can, but all the Java script and
things, search engines don’t like very much.
So, can you have an SEO optimized page that has a link to...
Jon: 	Yes.
Josh: 	I’m kind of curious here. Oh, he has it takes them to the VSL. Yeah.
Jon: 	Yes, yes. My friend, Mike Geary, I did his video sales letter. That was
the second one I ever did, after I did mine. His, if you go to

-- 3 of 15 --

4
TruthAboutAbs.com, you’ll see it. He has a splash page that says,
“Men click here; women click here,” which is really just – he wanted 2
different videos. So it’s not like an excuse to have the same video.
There are distinct differences in the 2 video sales letters.
But the powerful thing about that is he can do all the SEO tricks on that
page. So, yes, you can. Yes, you can.
Josh: 	Okay. And then, sort of a second part to this question is, “At what
point in the offer do you give them the ability to make their choice
between the different versions or products, and look at the info relative
to those products or versions?”
Jon: 	Again, I would suggest doing if you have different info, different
products, different selection, is where you’re walking through the price,
the drop-down stuff, obviously the drop down stuff isn’t going to work
because you have multiple price points, I assume.
So you’ve gone, you’re starting the grand offer, and you go through the
same steps that you would go through the grand offer, up until you get
to the price.
And at this point, rather than have the, “Normally, this is this much
money, but we’re going to do this today,” you can cut all of that. And
simply say, “Look below this video now,” and boom the entire sales
page appears, basically, “and choose the product option that fits best
for you.”
And then, if you have a specific product or a specific discount on
something say, “And I want you to notice the third product. It’s called
blank. We have a discount on that, just for watching this video.” Then,
you can use the same techniques that I talked about.
So, if you want to discount one of your things or if you discount all of
them and say, “Just for watching this video, everything you see has
already been discounted 10%,” you would have the real price and then
the discounted price.
So yeah, that’s where you would do it, right before you do the price
drop.
Josh: 	Okay, Jonas has a couple questions here. First is, “Jon, whenever you
first created your very first VSL, obviously you made more sales. But
did it also increase your refunds because maybe the customer feels
sold more so than a text sales copy?”
Jon: 	Nope. My refunds for the product has not budged. It’s also been
about 6%.

-- 4 of 15 --

5
Josh: 	Okay, I’m just going to read some. “Jon, can you make a list of things
to not do when making a video sales letter for a product that will be on
ClickBank, according to rules and regulations? Do you have any
ideas?”
Jon: 	I don’t need to do that, actually, because you just go to ClickBank.com
and they have the rules and regulations up there specifically for sales
pages and video sales letters. So, it’s already been done.
But, I can briefly tell you what they are. I mean it’s real simple.
You cannot have any over-inflated testimonials. In other words, you
cannot say, “Cindy lost 38 pounds using the Every Other Day Diet
System.” If she did lose 38 pounds, you have to specify like, “Cindy
followed the program to the letter, and she’s not like a lot of people
who downloaded the book and didn’t do anything with it. Now, this is
someone that actually worked for it.”
So, you have to kind of put those kind of caveats in there.
You can’t have a no-pause video. It means the video has to be able to
pause. And I think the only other stipulation with ClickBank is dealing
with recurring billing, which we’ve already talked about.
So, those are the 3 things that I know for sure that ClickBank is now
down on. I guess I just did make that video, because this is being
recorded. If there’s more, I’ll add to it; but not that I know of.
Josh: 	Okay, next Heather asks, “What’s the reason for putting in the Q&A at
the end? What’s the psychological reason for that?
Jon: 	Oh, it’s simple. The Q&A at the end, if anyone’s hanging on to the very
end, the reason why Q&A is always at the end of webinars as well, it’s
the same reason.
What we did, if you look carefully at the video sales letter, remember
the section in the very first segment, part one, where we say what it
isn’t, and we go through the it works even if? The even-if parts cover a
lot of the questions that people would ask. Like, “Would it work for me
if I had a thyroid condition? Would it work for me if I’ve been
overweight all my life?” those kind of things.
So, we’ve already covered that before we even got to the price. The
Q&A part is merely for the hangers-on. It’s for the people who are at
the very end, they’re watching, and the only reason they’re still
watching at that point is because they’re trying to decide if they’re
going to buy.

-- 5 of 15 --

6
They’re not watching for information. They know the information is
over. So, at that point, that’s the psychological reason for it. They’re
trying to figure out if they’re going to buy or not.
The best way to help somebody figure out if they’re going to buy is
answer their questions. So, if it was a webinar, you’d do it live. In a
video sales letter, you do it recorded.
Josh: 	Okay. So next question, from Richard, he’s asking, “Whenever you
have a front-end copy to get people to the VSL in the first place, is
there anything special like say a standard email campaign and sales
copy that you use for that?”
Jon: 	Yeah, the more I hear this, the more you guys really do need to go to
3X Conversion Formula, because we talk about all this stuff. I’ll give
you high-level stuff for sure, here, so don’t get me wrong. But it’s like
we have tutorial after tutorial on writing email copy and how to write
specific email copy for specific offers and landing pages.
But, the general idea behind email copy to send video sales letters is
make it super-short. You only want the click. When you have a sales
page, you need to qualify that click. because they’re going to read War
And Peace. Right? Well, a video sales letter, they’re not expecting
that.
So, you use the word presentation, free presentation. You talk about
the 3 things it’s going to cover. And then in your PS, bring out one
thing that you really love the best. Say it’s something like, “About 6
minutes into the presentation, Jon covers this really awesome tip for
weight loss that really helped me out, so be sure to watch the whole
thing,” or something to that effect.
That’s the basics of it. Just short, to the point, link, PS, link.
Josh: 	Okay. Richard is wondering, “Is there a particular reason you want

Keynote, rather than finishing the whole script in Word?”
Jon: 	Okay, I’m being a little bit of a hard-ass here. Yeah, you can do the

words, the homework for this section, this was so detailed. The other
sections were detailed, too. We tried to cover everything. And, you’ve
probably noticed – gravy – tons of detail.
But, this one was so broken down, step by step, that there’s no point in
cut-and-pasting and putting into Word every section that you’re
supposed to do, when it’s already right there for you in the slides and

7
in the mind maps. I’m just basically going to say, “Look at the mind

You’re more than happy to create that in Word, you’re just going to
have to cut-and-paste all those slides over to PowerPoint.
Now, we are going to give our really wonky Word-to-PowerPoint import
technique. In fact, I was going to get with Josh after we do this. We’re
going to record it here, ourselves, after we’re through, because it is so
wonky. PowerPoint is just retarded in the sense that it won’t let you,
for whatever reason import, like Keynote does.
So, if you’re using Keynote, by chance, write the whole thing in Word,
import it, boom, you’ve got all your slides. It’s really nice. PowerPoint,
not so good.
But we have a workaround. It’s just not super-elegant, but it works.
So, I’ll give you that workaround.
So, yes, you can do your homework in Word, if you really want to. It’s
just going to be easier. And honestly, there’s a little bit of psychology
here. I’m trying to get you guys into PowerPoint; because this is about
30% of your video sales letter. If you have it in PowerPoint, chances
are you’re going to do the rest of your video sales letter.
I want to see you guys succeed. I want to see you guys have a
finished video sales letter up, live, making money, and then you start
tweaking it.
That’s been my focal point from the word go, is to get you guys
something you can start using right now.
So, if you really want to do it in Word, great. I recommend writing the
stories in Word and writing the outline in Word. But this, again, is so
outlined and broken out.
But again, I’m just going to give you guys the option. You can do it in
Word, but the homework is not going to be Word assignments. The
homework is going to be, “Look at your mind map, look at the slides
we’ve uploaded, take part one, and do it.” That’s the homework.
There’s nothing more specific I can say that I didn’t say in the slides,
unlike the other bits of homework. Does that make sense? Is that cool
with everybody?
Josh: 	I think people probably think that’s cool. I’ll tell you in a minute.
David asked a question. He’s saying that he’s got a programmer, and

you keep mentioning programmers for certain things because you will

Jon: 	Yeah, that’s ridiculous. Go to Elance or Guru.com and post up there.

Plus, that is an added benefit of being in the same time zone and being

right now, are working for like $5 an hour; ridiculously cheap. A lot of
guys in Hong Kong, they’re excellent programmers. In fact, one would

But you don’t need a programmer of that caliber to do this. In fact, you

That’s what we promised, and that’s what the reality of the situation is.
But you do need a programmer if you’re going to say, “Okay, I want

Jon: 	Of course, it is. I’m one of the biggest guys on ClickBank. Not to brag

The only issues are what we covered earlier; you have to have a video

Josh: 	Alright, so next question is, “Can the 2 parts of PowerPoint, like you

9
have 2 different sections of PowerPoint, can it be merged into one
document, the PowerPoint?”
Jon: 	Yeah. I’m going to show you how to do that right now, because let’s

Okay, let’s just pretend that this, right here, was my video sales letter,

open up a different document. I’ll open steps 3 through 4. It doesn’t

And let’s say that I want to copy these slides right over here. So

Okay, I’m over here. Look at the view I’m in. I’m in this tab view right
here. I’m under the slides view. I’m under home. I’m just going to
hold down the shift key, copy. I want to copy these 4 slides. I could
copy 10-million, it doesn’t matter. I hit command C. I come over here,
and if I’ve got this slide selected, it’s going to paste it right under this
slide, by hitting command B. Boom. It’s that simple.
Presto-chango, you’ve got your whole thing done in 2 seconds. Same
exact thing for Keynote, too. Cut, paste. Very, very easy. Does that

Josh: 	That did for me. It’s pretty good. So, Jonas says another question.
He says, “For the magic buy button that pops up, is it better to just
have the button pop up by itself, or to pop up the sales page text?”
Jon: 	That, again, is a testing question. I’ve done it. First of all, we just did
the button, obviously.
I have heard that, from several people in specific niches, that having a
sales page underneath your entire video sales letter increased their
conversions. I find that very, very interesting. I have tried that, and it
did not increase my conversions. So, it may be a niche-specific thing.
So, here’s the test you want to run. First test, video sales letter with
magic buy button. That’s it.
Second test, video sales letter with pause, show the sales page, and
video sales letter. Same thing; if you pause it or if you end it, it shows
the sales page. And, video sales letter with the entire sales page
underneath it the entire time. Those are the 3 tests.
But, again, that’s advanced stuff. The most important thing, right now,
is to get you guys to get a video sales letter up, use the magic buy
button, and worry about the testing once it’s up, once you have

numbers. Because if you start guessing, you’re probably wrong. That

Jon: 	Yeah, you can do that. I use TextSoap, but it’s 6 of one, half-dozen of

looking for it by email.
Josh: 	Okay, Jonas has just a quick question I think you’ll be able to clarify.
He says, “Are you supposed to just follow the steps in the PowerPoint
slides that he has, or is in 2 parts?” he says. I think he’s just a little bit
confused about creating. I think he came on later or didn’t come to
every call. What advice do you have for him to sort of tackle this in the
best way possible?
Jon: 	If I understand your question, you’ve got to go through the USP factory
and in the 1-2-3 formula. You got to do that first. There’s no if, ands,
or buts. After you get through those, the homework sessions, then
you’re going to get to the 5 steps. And each of the 5 steps have a
PowerPoint slide associated with them.
We covered steps one and 2 in one session. So, that’s got one
PowerPoint slide. Steps 3 and 4 was last week. And step 5 is now.
So, you have 3 total PowerPoint files that you will use the technique I
just showed you a while ago, to cut, copy, paste, and put them all into
one file when you’re done.
Josh: 	This next one’s kind of funny. Just a side note. Richard said that his
wife was walking in and heard some of the slides that you were using
as examples for your weight loss thing, and she wants to buy it now.
Jon: 	Testimonial to the fact that wordsmithing works. Thanks. I won’t make
her watch the entire video, she can just email me. I’ll send her a link.
Yeah, this stuff is really cool. Let me end by saying this.
The things that I try to emphasize in copy is, and this is probably like a

-- 13 of 15 --

1
4
little beyond the scope of what we’re wanting to cover, but I think it will
help you guys out, is I want to emphasize like real-world stuff, like
make it as honest and down-to-earth as possible while, at the same
time, making the benefits just outrageous.
Because, if you have a great product and someone does the product
correctly, for whatever lack of better words, they’re going to get
outrageous benefits.
I bought 500 books. But say somebody sold me one book back when I
was obese, in my 30’s, and sold me one book on bodybuilding and
weight loss, and it told me everything that I know right now. And they
were saying, “Okay, value is great at $47, but here’s what’s going to
happen. You’re going to blah-blah-blah,” and did all these amazing
future-pacing kind of benefits, every single one of those, plus 1,000
more, would have been true. Absolutely true.
So, that’s how I approach writing copy. I approach writing copy from if
someone does what I tell them to do and applies themselves 100%,
this is what’s going to happen.
That’s how you approach copy. And that also keeps you away from
the temptation to exaggerate or lie or whatever. There’s lots of
marketers who do that. I’m not going to BS you guys about that. We
don’t have to. You don’t. If you’ve got a good product, you don’t have
to. And if you’re a good wordsmither, you don’t have to. You really
don’t.
So, if you have a product that can change someone’s life, you need to
tell them. You need to tell them.
That’s where I emphasize. A lot of marketers come across, the
exaggeration comes from how much money they’ll make, that kind of
thing, from like internet marketers.
Again, you guys already know this, because you’ve heard it for weeks
now, I emphasize the benefits. I emphasize the senses and the
feelings and the benefits, because that’s what you’re buying. That’s
what people want.
I agree with the whole sell someone what they think they need and
give them what they really need. I understand that. I totally get it, and
it makes sense.
But you can do both vy simply saying, “Yeah, you’ll make more money.
Yeah, you’ll have all that. But you know what you really need? It’s
what we all really need. We want more peace, more freedom. And so
you can do both. You can do both.”

-- 14 of 15 --

1
5
So that’s my pep talk for writing copy. Just think about that when
you’re writing your own close and you’re writing your own grand offer,
you’re writing your own video sales letter in general. You want to be
thinking in terms of like what is the best benefits possible that this can
render to someone, that truly applies it, without exaggeration, and write
focused on that.
Look at Richard’s wife, what just happened. Walking by, hearing a few
slides, “I want to buy it.” None of that that was exaggerated.
It was the benefits. I could really go further. And I could go a lot
further with the pain, too – a lot further, because I’ve been through it.
I’ve been through both.
So, that’s really what I want to inspire you guys to do, is to write about
the pain, and write about the pleasure. Emphasize those aspects.
And then when you do talk about the products, just talk about how,
“You get this, and on this page you’re going to learn this, and on this
page you’re going to learn this.” People never buy a thing; they buy a
solution. Does that make sense?
So, if you focus in terms of solution, you’ll have it made.
Alright, cool. I think that this was a great Q&A, and I think this was a
great call.
We will send you out the link for Friday’s call, the bonus call. It’s the
same time, 11:00 a.m. And we’ll probably go until 1:00-ish, maybe
2:00. But that’s going to be a lot of fun, because we’re going to get
you guys on the phone with us. So, come prepared.
And guys, girls, do your homework. Make this happen. Set aside the
time it takes to do this. If it’s an hour a day, set it aside, half an hour a
day, whatever it is, you can do this. And I guarantee you, if you do it
and complete this thing and you get it online, it’s going to be one of the
best feelings you’ll ever have, because you’ll get a huge feeling of
accomplishment.
